speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Friday, August 11, 1995. The survivor is Frances Jones. Interviewer-- Tama Freund. City, state-- Encino, California, United States of America. And the language is English. Hello, my name is Tama Freund-- T-A-M-A F-R-E-U-N-D. The date today is Friday August 11, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Frances Weinberg Jones in Encino, California, in the United States of America. Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my name is Frances Weinberg Jones. And I live in Los Angeles, in Encino, California, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your name for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jones. It's J-O-N-E-S. Weinberg is W-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth is March, 22, 1924, in Cologne, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Cologne for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-O-L-O-G-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'm 71 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe life in Cologne before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came from a very nice Jewish family. My father was a businessman. And we were surrounded by a large amount of relatives. It was a very warm, nice Jewish household. I have a sister. My sister is five years younger than I. I had a brother, who died when I was three years old. I don't remember him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Josef-- J-O-S-E-F-- Weinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amalie-- A-M-A-L-I-E-- maiden name-- [? Mutzner, ?] Weinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is Margot Arden-- A-R-D-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your daily life for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daily life was we went to school. We always went to a Jewish day school. And we had a very nice life with my parents. We used to travel a lot, because Cologne was situated near the Rhine. So there were a lot of beautiful places to go to. And it was a very Jewish home, and lots of friends and cousins. So that's my background. It was a good home. It was a nice-- it was a nice time to look back on. I had very warm and good parents who took good care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was religion like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- you would not refer to really very orthodox-- I would say almost like a conservative home. We kept a [NON-ENGLISH] home, because my grandparents used to come. And we observed all the holidays and the Shabbat. And we would go to temple. And I remember temple was really nice. You meet your friends. And it was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would your mother make for Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she would make gefilte fish-- I'm sure you know that-- and chicken soups, and chicken, and meats, and potatoes. It-- you know, it was the traditional Shabbat dinner. And it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the name. It was a small synagogue that was more or less created by our family. It was next door to the big synagogue. And we were maybe 50 or 60 families. And so it was very intimate. We had a wonderful cantor and rabbi. But I don't remember the name, what they actually called the synagogue. So I don't. But it was a very nice-- nice place to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any hobbies or interests outside of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was younger-- well, I used to love roller skating. I was very good roller skater. I used to ice skate, sled when the snow came. And-- and generally, I had a lot of gymnastics classes. I loved gym. I was a good runner. And going to the movies, watching all the Shirley Temple movies. So there was [INAUDIBLE]. And between then, we had to do our homework, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- don't forget, I was in elementary school. And we-- in those days, we did not study foreign languages. I did take private lessons, [NON-ENGLISH], because my father was a big Zionist. And he was with the Maccabi organization, president. And so we had always hoped to go to Palestine. So I had private lessons in [NON-ENGLISH]. So it kept me-- I kept pretty busy as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the Zionist movement in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very big movement in Cologne, as I remember. There were the [? Hakoah ?] organization, which was the gym group from the girls. And we used to have meetings, and sing, you know, like-- and learn songs-- and kept us out of trouble. No, it was a very good Zionist organization. Because I was sort of-- I grew up in that milieu, in a very Zionistic milieu.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first became aware of anti-Semitism in Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really as a-- as a child, in the beginning, I did not. I was not aware of it, until I saw, one day walking to my aunt, men in brown shirts and a swastika. But-- so I asked my dad. And he says, oh, it's nothing. They're just a group of people. They'll go away. We'll go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, no. The actually problem started when Kristallnacht came. That's when I really became aware of anti-Semitism. I remember, though, there were stores that you could not shop. So there were a lot of Jewish stores open and Jewish coffee houses, which was very popular, and dancing. So it never really occurred to me. Because I never had the problem myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I played in the street with the kids. And so I really think back later-- Cologne, for many years, it was not as bad as other cities were. Cologne-- it came quite a bit later. For some reason, the Cologne people were a bit more tolerant. But it-- when Kristallnacht came, that's when I really became aware of I was a Jew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and we were different, that we were different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before then, did you play-- did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. We were allowed to play in the streets. It's a quiet street-- oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went to Jewish day school. The first time I ever went to a non-Jewish school, public school, was when I arrived in France, but not in Germany. We could not go to Christian school. We had to go to Jewish day school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean you were not allowed to go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of your schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the name-- the first school I went to was [GERMAN] School. And then later on, I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. That was sort of a secondary school-- also a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorites subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah-- history and geography. I was always fascinated by that. That was really my favorite. I mean, I was dreaming. And I used to love to look at maps and places where I want to be, where I want to go. So these really were my very favorite subjects in school. And I think they're still today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your home, where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home-- we lived in an apartment. Unless you live in the outskirts of Cologne, you didn't have private houses. You have-- you had like in Paris. You have apartment buildings, big apartment building. And we had a very nice apartment. It was pretty big. And I know that we had a lot of books, because my mother was an avid reader. She loved to read, my mother. So there were always books around the house. And my father was more interested in listening to short wave radio and sport. And so that was sort of his thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own bedroom? Or did you share?

speaker: interviewee
text: I shared it with my sister. Yeah, we had our bedroom. Mm hm. So it was pleasant to think back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first signs of danger in your co--

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in '38 at Kristallnacht. They came-- I saw them coming down our street. And I kind of told my mom. And she quick closed all the windows and put the blinds down. And upstairs lived a Jewish family above us. And they came to our building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole group of Nazi. I mean, they were not all in uniform. But they came with big sticks and just-- I don't know what they all had in their hands. And they went upstairs first. And they just totally demolished the apartment-- threw down into the street. Then they knocked on our door. Fortunately, my father was not home. And they asked where my father was. And my mother-- he's not home. Come in and look. And they says, no. It's OK. And they left. It was kind of the interesting. Across the street from us lived a couple. They were-- he was an SS man. But he always liked my father. He was always buddy with my father. And so they never, in the beginning, didn't touch us at all. So-- but at that point, I really became aware things were really bad. Fortunately, we had a woman who were helping my mother in the house. And she would go shopping for us and bring food in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father the night of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: That night, he was someplace out of town. I don't quite remember where he was. But he was out of town. And he was hidden by whomever he-- where he was. They hid him that day. So he didn't get back to Cologne. Because apparently, it happened there too, wherever he was. So he knew something was going on. And he did not come back til the following night. And at that point, he spoke with my mother. They spoke kind of quietly. I didn't know what they were talking about. But what it was-- they made plans that my father would leave. He hired a guide. See, really, Cologne was very close to Holland and also to Belgium. So he would leave-- he hired a guide and would go on to Holland, to Amsterdam. And he told my mother that he would send somebody for her. But please, first to send us, my sister and I, away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother called my uncle in France. They-- he had-- my two uncles, [INAUDIBLE] brothers, had lived in France for many, many years. They went there as very young men and were French citizens. And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle was-- the one that picked us up was my Uncle Max. He was a twin. And then my Uncle [? Sammy. ?] And Max came and picked my sister and I up. And I remember when the taxi came, and the suitcases were taken out, my friend from next door asked me, hey, where are you going? So-- and I turned to her and I says, I'm going to America. So in the taxi, my mom said, why did you say that? I says, well, felt that if I say Paris, it was too close. So you know, I wanted to kind of say I'm going far away. Because I knew America was far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go with your parents? Why did you and your sister split up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you had to have papers. You had to have visas to go from one country to another, unless you go illegally, like my father did, over the border to Holland. And ultimately, my mother had to do the same thing, where you pay for a guide, and they take you across the border. You didn't get any more visas to leave the country. That was the whole thing. And-- because people were lining up on consulates all over Cologne, trying to get visas to god knows where. And you couldn't get them anymore. So-- and that was the reason that my mother had to send us away. And now that I'm a mother and a grandmother, I really could feel now how she must have felt saying goodbye to us, not knowing if, where, when she would see us again. But when you're younger, you don't think about this-- not til later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, when we arrived in Paris, I had my aunt there and my uncle waiting for us. It was a little difficult. Because neither one of my sister or I could not speak French. And my aunt only spoke French. My uncles could speak German. So it was very difficult to communicate. So there was an organization in Paris called the Ose-- O-S-E. And they were part of the joint-- highest joint-- organization. And they suggested to my uncle that they had this children's home and that we should be put in one of these children's home-- children our age-- and we would learn French much easier. So my sister went into one home that was run by the [? Rothschild ?] family. They gave a lot of money. And I was sent to another home. And we started-- I started to have my schooling. But my French-- I didn't learn fast enough. Because there were a lot of children from different countries-- Austria and others. So we kind of spoke German together. Were these homes for Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish children. It was very nice. It was beautiful homes they put us. And it was not like an orphanage at all. It was like-- almost like a private school house. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember about the date that you came to this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1939. Yeah, that's when I came to France, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you treated as a Jew? Were you openly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in France, I had no problem. No. There was no problem, no. In 1930 and-- France hasn't been in the war yet. And the French, very frankly, hated the Germans. I mean, that was their worst enemies. So no, we had no problem. I presume there are individuals-- maybe they're anti-Semitic. But lots of Jews lived in Paris and in France. And you know, they lived like French people. So there was no problem there. And-- but what would happened at the home where I was-- my uncle and aunt came to see me. And they did not like the idea my French wasn't coming fast enough. So they had a-- they knew of a family, a wonderful family, that lived in what they call [SPEAKING FRENCH]. It's in the province of the Champagne. And they wanted to take a foster child in, a Jewish foster child. And so my uncle took me there with the premise that if I don't like it, I could go back. You know-- I wasn't sent there, if I have to stay. But they were just wonderful. And there were, at that time when I got there, five children, plus me. And I learned French very, very fast. Because that's what I had to do. There was just nobody else I could speak to. So when you have to, and you're young, you do pick up a language very fast. And I stayed with them. Then the war broke out. And we decided to leave the village. Because we lived too close to the German border there. And we went to central France, where Mr. Barzanges had come from originally. It's part of the Auvergne, in France-- a very pretty area-- near Vichy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there-- So what year was that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was late '39, 40-- around that time-- right in the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you left-- before the family moved, what did you do with them besides--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school there, with the rest of the kids. And I was just treated like their daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, they did know that I was a refugee, a German Jewish refugee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in public school? And in

speaker: interviewee
text: Pub-- yeah, there was no problem. Uh uh. When the war broke out, we had a problem. Because we were bombed. And because we were so close to the German border and that whole area, that's why Mr. Barzanges decided we should leave the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-A-R-Z-A-N-G-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: His first name is Victor Barzange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left. And we went to Clermont-Ferrand. It was sort of an interesting journey. Because we were in an old car with 14 people in the car. We were standing, sitting on running boards. And well, when you're young, we managed. And the German soldiers-- going in, cussing every day, because all the civilians were running. And we ended up arriving in town, in Clermont-Ferrand, the same day as the Germans had arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got there. And it was sort of an interesting episode for me. He sent us to the post office-- so two of his older children and I, to get stamps, I presume. And we walked into the post office. And there were two German officers standing there. And I looked at the one. And he looked at me. And he called my name. He says, hello Fanny. And it was my neighbor. How are you? How is your family? And I was panicked. And I ran. It took me two hours to find the apartment where I'm was supposed to be. But it was sort of a funny experience for me. Because I was afraid that the Germans-- for the Germans. And I was afraid the French kind of kept looking. Who is she, you know, talking to this German officer? And then I remember, it was that neighbor, that SS man, that really was awful friendly to us and to my dad. And he-- I had to see him, yep. At this point, did you know anything of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not know. A little bit later, I found out that my uncle had written to them and told them where I was with, with that family. And they knew the first place where I was, in [FRENCH]. And they would write to them. But then the war broke out. And we just lost contact. And after a while, I resumed-- when I went to a different home, then I found mail from my parents, where they were put into a camp in Westerbork. And they were able to write to me. So in those days, I still had heard occasionally from them. I think they were allowed to write once a month. So-- and so we stayed in Clermont-Ferrand. And then Mr. Barzange decided that they need to go back to their own village. But he didn't want to take me. So he found somebody by the name of Mr. Baer-- B-A-E-R, I think, and that he run a Jewish organization. So he took me there. And he promised this Mr. Barzange that they would-- he would take me, and take care of me, and take me to a home, and I would be safe. And he took me on a bus, a couple hours away from Clermont-Ferrand, in the mountains someplace. I don't know the village or anything about it. And the people-- there were a lot of Jewish people there-- I mean, not a lot. I would say maybe 100. And you slept in big dormitories on cots. And they had rationed out the food. We had very little food-- one little piece of bread and some soup, and very little. And I noticed that people were-- every day people were going away. And I don't know where they went to. And there were always less people. And then people started to talk. They weren't sure where they were, and where the other people were gone. And I remember the lady that sent me originally to the Ose home, that she was in Limoges. And I wrote to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a lady that you had met--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met her in Paris. She's the one that sent me originally to the Ose home-- this woman, Mrs. [? Grunewalt. ?] And I wrote to her. And I ask her if I please could come to her, that I was afraid. This was a funny place, and I don't want to stay here. And she immediately sent me money and a pass. Because you had to have passes to go from one county to the next. And she's says, get on the bus and come here, which I did. And I stayed in Limoges with her for a while. And then she sent me to another home she felt would be better for me, because there were children my age-- you know, young people my age. And that was Chateau [? de ?] [? Chabon. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, did you ever find out or hear of anything that happened at that place, where you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the people were disappearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No more, uh uh. I never heard anything about it. It just like-- I just can't explain what happened. I just don't know. All I know, I wanted to get away. And I couldn't get away fast enough from there. So I ended up in [? Chabon. ?] And it was a very nice home. And I met people there that to this day we are friends. They're like my brothers and sisters. We get together for reunions. And we're just a very close-knit bunch. And while I was in [? Chabon, ?] at that point, that was in 1941, when I first came there. The director of the home asked me-- I had my sister there now, in [? Chabon. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a home for Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, strictly Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there schooling there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry. Was there schooling in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- we had classes every day-- every day. And the rest, we just-- took care of our own-- our own needs. But we had school every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- yes, we had fairly good food, yeah-- at that point, yes. And he asked me if my-- they have a chance. The Quakers were taking younger children to the States. Would I let Margot, my sister, go. I says, please, yes. I knew I had family in the States, in New York. And they said that she would come to New York, but that she was assigned to a foster family to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, I didn't care. All I knew-- I wanted her to go to America. And I stayed on in [? Chabon. ?] And then one day, I was asked-- there was a girl there, [? Angele, ?] I remember. I still see her occasionally. Her name was [? Angele ?] Haas-- H-A-A-S. And she had a sister and-- a younger sister and brother in another home, in southern France, right by the Spanish border, in the Pyrenees. Would I like to change and go there? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so I said yes. Got it.
